replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@dontgiveinfo 
*"A kid trains for 3 months and he can take on daredevil?"*
Right? MCU Daredevil is apparently just a blind lawyer in a silly suit: He has a hard time beating an angsty teen with 3 months of Taekwondo "training" in a kids class and an out of shape, female amputee with the "Power of One, the Power of Many"...to make you feel feelings.

Peak characterization, all the way around...

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
@Tony-hv8wz 
*"Yall do realize that in the comics muse has some level of enhanced strength right?"*
You do realize that this was an MCU show on D+ and not a comic book, right? If the MCU has done one thing *VERY* well since the first _Iron Man_ film, it's demonstrate that the characters in the films and series are *NOT* the same characters as those in the comics. 

*"He's a meta."*
in the comics. Did anyone explain that in this show? Is that information even _implied_ by this mess?

*"That might explain it"*
And that makes it a plot hole. If the audience has to create their own explanation for anything in a story that is completely contrary to the facts established within the story's universe, it's a plot hole and evidence of terrible writing. Within the context established by the _Daredevil_ television series, both on Netflix AND D+, it makes absolutely no sense that the Muse in _Born Again_ (again) would last more than about 3 seconds in a fight with Daredevil. Per the explanation given _by this show,_ Muse took a few months of Taekwondo lessons as a kid/teen, and he was then able to go toe-to-toe with Daredevil. 

If you have to justify completely nonsensical depictions in a story by creating your own narrative, the storyteller has failed.

replies.text: ​@Prominent_intellectto quote Random Film Talk: "if a show or film fails to make sense merely on the grounds that you have read the source material, it has failed as a production". These aren't made just so the people who have read the book can have a second chance to see it all on screen, it needs to work for people who have never been into it before. Even a sequel ought to be able to stand alone if you haven't seen the previous films, although that's arguably less important, since you can argue that the whole series was supposed to be consumed together, like volumes in a book. You don't need to read the Silmarilion to read the Lord of the Rings, but you can't read The Two Towers without reading Fellowship. You can watch the Jackson trilogy just fine without reading either. Because it was written by people who know what they are doing.

replies.text:
@kicknowledgesmith8608 
*"It was never Netflix property, it was always Disney."*
I don't know if you're the same genius I've seen posting this on multiple videos, but you are absolutely, blatantly, 100% wrong. And it only takes about 30 seconds of searching to find out how incredibly wrong you are. 

*"Kevin Feige and Bob Igor produced it. "*
LOL. Nope. I've looked at the credits for every season of Netflix's _Daredevil,_ many times. Neither of those two clowns are credited as producers. You could look up the credited producers for the series, too. If you did (and if you had someone read them to you), you would see that neither Feige nor Iger are credited as producers. You could also look into the company that owned the television rights for Daredevil during the Netflix run. If you did (and if you had someone read the information and explain it to you), you'd see that Disney objectively did NOT have any ownership of those rights, and that they were in no way affiliated with the show's production. But don't let facts and reality get in the way of your delusions, I guess.

Additionally, Bob Iger has exactly TWO producer credits to his name: "Disney Legends Award Ceremony," and "D23: Secrets, Celebrations and More!" That's it. He has never been a producer on ANY MCU project. (You should REALLY try to do even a modicum of research into a topic before you start making wild claims that are easily disproven. You apparently have the intellectual capacity of a flat Earther, because you're just screeching nonsense.)

replies.author: @sarahcashburger1560
replies.text:
@ Counterpoint. It makes no sense for Spider-Man to shoot itself in the foot by referencing this disaster that nobody watched. Just as this show should have ignored Echo and Hawkeye and She-Hulk, Spider-Man should ignore this. I hate this idea that just because there are crossovers no one should have their individual story. 

Marvel comics itself worked that way from its genesis and for decades upon decades, different stories were in the same universe, hell the same city, but most of the time the heroes didn't acknowledge each other's escapades. 

And even if there isn't precedent for doing things this way, again, why should they acknowledge bad product? The show sucked, it didn't work, it doesn't make sense to dredge it up and say 'hey audience, remember this thing that sucked? no? Well too bad it's integral to the plot now'. That's what they did for Captain Falcon and dredging up Incredible Hulk and Falcon and Winter Soldier characters nobody gives a shit about, and see how that worked out for them.

replies.author: @JBrotsis1
@sarahcashburger1560
replies.text:
@ While the show's quality is certainly subjective, the ratings (or lack thereof) are not. Why bring in something unpopular? As for your point on other unpopular things being brought back, yes the MCU has certainly done it before, will certainly do it again, but that doesn't make it a good idea. Bringing back the Marvels will be a bad idea when it happens, as will likely bringing back Ant-Man in the Avengers, just as it was a bad idea when Captain Falcon did it for unpopular Incredible Hulk characters. As for Thor while the movie did not meet expectations, it didn't bomb like all the rest of these examples did, which I think places it in its own category. 

Also I disagree with this notion. I see what you're saying on its face, but the problem with doing this is it invalidates individual stories by forcing everything to be a crossover or to have low stakes. Under your logic, either it has high stakes and *has* to involve the other heroes in the same universe, completely killing the solo story as a concept because every hero in this universe pretty much lives in walking distance minus like two exceptions, which would mean every project is an Avengers movie, or the story has to have such low stakes that it wouldn't attract the attention of other heroes which is just lame and crippling to creative storytelling.

Also I simply don't understand your assertion that the mcu has somehow been connected to this level up to this point. It became a meme, but where are the Avengers in Thor 2, Iron Man 3, Captain America 2? By your logic the consequences should have carried out into the larger MCU, but they didn't really. The entirety of Thor 2 was pretty much shadow-exorcised from canon until Infinity War/Endgame with the unceremonious removal of Jane, Shield being Hydra really didn't have any effects in any other movies aside from that being presumably how Strucker got the scepter, and Iron Man had a brand new bunch of suits in Avengers 2, completely undercutting the entirety of Iron Man 3. And there are a billion other examples (for another example the Ego clump in Missouri that's never mentioned in another film). So let's not pretend that these films were ever super connected to each other.
